The value they get in the first six months will be like... yeah. The value they'll have in six years time if they keep up with it will be wild. Those people won't just be users, they'll be like fans and advocates.
Academies have their local towns, regions, catchment areas locked the fuck down. And that is why you can reliably generate talent. If you aren't able to do that, you don't survive as an academy.
The company that owns a university's career pipeline owns 10 years of talent flow from that institution - territorial lock-in through early relationship building
Companies will never invest in true AI for resume scanning. Because why would you process a resource where the ratio is like 99 crap for 1% signal. And even that could be totally fake now.
AI didn't improve talent assessment; it may have made it worse. Information asymmetry is increasing, not decreasing. The ATS is a permanent ceiling, not a problem to solve.

source_display: Careerspan B2C to Hiring Side Pivot
evidence: Going B2C to job seekers under duress was problematic... Pivot to HIRING side: realized they've built a high-signal community. ~3-4K users, 1/3+ with multiple stories. Deep data unlike any other platform.
Ivor suggested Handshake unprompted; V confirmed it's the actual perfect acquirer. Existing product-market fit with career centers; Handshake has distribution to all colleges.
Ivor's new Head of Strategic Ops will reach out after joining (week after next) to pursue Handshake introduction

Whoever builds the career OS for a generation of college students and maintains that relationship for a decade controls the most valuable dataset in the labor market—one that can't be faked because it's built on years of authentic interaction.
If all three ideas are true (longitudinal infrastructure, territorial lock-in, broken assessment), then Careerspan's asymmetric bet on career OS makes sense
Synthesis: longitudinal career data + territorial lock-in + broken traditional assessment = career OS as the only defensible play
